title: Java Operators

introduction.what_it_is: Operators in Java are special symbols that perform specific operations on one, two, or three operands, and then return a result. They are the foundation of any programming language used to manipulate variables and values.
introduction.why_important: Understanding operators is crucial because they allow you to perform mathematical, relational, and logical computations. Without operators, a program cannot process data or make decisions.
real_world: Calculating the total price of items in an e-commerce cart (Arithmetic).
Checking if a user is over 18 years old to allow access (Relational).
Verifying both username and password are correct for login (Logical).

detailed_theory: Java provides a rich set of operators to manipulate variables. Operators can be classified based on the number of operands they take: Unary (one operand), Binary (two operands), and Ternary (three operands). Furthermore, based on their functionality, they are categorized into Arithmetic, Relational, Logical, Bitwise, Assignment, and Shift operators. Each operator has a specific precedence and associativity rule which dictates the order of evaluation in an expression.

features.name: Precedence
features.description: Determines the order in which operators in an expression are evaluated (e.g., multiplication before addition).

features.name: Associativity
features.description: Determines the direction of evaluation (left-to-right or right-to-left) when operators of the same precedence appear in an expression.

features.name: Overloading
features.description: In Java, operators cannot be overloaded by the user, unlike in C++. However, the '+' operator is overloaded internally for string concatenation.

advantages: Provides concise syntax for complex operations (e.g., ternary operator).
Bitwise operators allow low-level data manipulation, increasing performance for specific tasks.
limitations: Lack of operator overloading can make custom mathematical classes (like Matrices or Complex numbers) verbose.
Incorrect use of precedence can lead to logical errors that are hard to debug.
Bitwise operations can be difficult to read and maintain for beginners.

syntax.explanation: Operators are typically placed between operands (binary), before or after an operand (unary), or involve three parts separated by '?' and ':' (ternary).
rules: Operands must be of compatible types.
Division by integer zero throws ArithmeticException.
Logical operators (&&, ||) support short-circuiting, meaning the second operand is only evaluated if necessary.

examples.title: Arithmetic Operators
examples.explanation: Demonstrates basic arithmetic operations including modulo which gives the remainder.

examples.title: Relational and Logical Operators
examples.explanation: Uses the >= relational operator to check age and the && logical operator to combine two conditions. Both must be true.

examples.title: Bitwise and Shift Operators
examples.output:
Bitwise AND: 1
Bitwise OR: 7
Bitwise XOR: 6
Left Shift x by 1: 10
examples.explanation: Bitwise operators work on individual bits. 5 & 3 is 0101 & 0011 = 0001 (1). Left shifting 5 by 1 multiplies it by 2 (5 * 2^1 = 10).

comparisons.explanation: Bitwise AND operates on bits and evaluates both sides regardless. Logical AND operates on booleans and short-circuits (skips evaluating the right side if the left side is false).

methods.name: Ternary Operator (?:)
methods.return_value: Returns expression1 if true, else expression2.

mistakes.title: Using '=' instead of '=='
mistakes.description: Confusing the assignment operator (=) with the equality relational operator (==) in conditions.
mistakes.debugging_tip: Java compiler will throw a 'type mismatch' error if you assign an int inside an if condition, but it compiles if it's a boolean variable. Always double-check equality checks.

basic.question: What is the difference between ++a and a++?
basic.answer: ++a (pre-increment) increments the value first, then uses it in the expression. a++ (post-increment) uses the current value in the expression, then increments it.

intermediate.question: Explain short-circuit logical operators.
intermediate.answer: && and || are short-circuit operators. For &&, if the left operand is false, the overall result must be false, so the right operand is not evaluated. For ||, if the left operand is true, the overall result must be true, so the right operand is not evaluated.

advanced.question: What is the unsigned right shift operator (>>>)?
advanced.answer: The >>> operator shifts the bits to the right and always fills the leftmost bits with zero, regardless of the sign bit. The >> operator (signed right shift) preserves the sign bit (fills with 1 for negative numbers, 0 for positive).

easy: Write a program to calculate the area of a rectangle using arithmetic operators.
Use relational operators to compare two numbers and print the larger one.
medium: Write a program to check if a year is a leap year using logical operators.
Use the ternary operator to find the maximum of three numbers.
hard: Swap two numbers without using a third variable, using bitwise XOR (^).
Write a program to count the number of set bits (1s) in an integer using bitwise operators.

coding_challenges.statement: Given an integer `n`, determine if it is a power of two using a single line of code and bitwise operators.

real_world_applications.explanation: Bitwise XOR is extensively used in encryption algorithms to scramble data.
Bitwise shift operators are used to set, clear, or toggle specific bits in hardware registers.

best_practices: Use parentheses to explicitly define the order of evaluation if you are unsure of operator precedence.
Prefer logical short-circuit operators (&&, ||) over bitwise (&, |) for boolean conditions to avoid NullPointerExceptions.
Keep expressions simple; do not chain too many operators in a single line.
summary: Operators are fundamental symbols used to perform operations on operands.
They are categorized into Arithmetic, Relational, Logical, Assignment, Bitwise, Unary, and Ternary.
Understanding operator precedence and associativity is critical for writing bug-free expressions.
Short-circuit operators improve efficiency and prevent runtime errors in certain scenarios.

quiz.question: What is the output of `System.out.println(10 + 20 + "J");`?
quiz.explanation: Evaluation is from left to right. 10 + 20 is evaluated mathematically to 30. Then 30 + "J" becomes string concatenation, resulting in "30J".

quiz.question: Which operator is used to perform unsigned right shift?
quiz.explanation: The unsigned right shift operator `>>>` shifts zero into the leftmost position regardless of the sign.
